items.foco: Biological cycle with snail as intermediate host
items.title: Biological cycle
items.contentHtml:
Biological cycle

  • Snail of the genus Biomphalaria (S. mansoni) or Bulinus (S. haematobium) is the obligate intermediate host.
  • Embryonated eggs release miracidium that penetrates the snail; there it transforms into a sporocyst and produces cercariae.
  • Cercariae swim free in fresh water and penetrate intact human skin.
  • Inside the human they become schistosomuli, they migrate through pulmonary circulation to the portal system (S. mansoni) or bladder plexus (S. haematobium).
  • Adults copulate and females release eggs in venules; the cycle requires fresh water contaminated with feces/urine.

items.foco: Schistosoma mansoni causes intestinal and hepatosplenic form
items.title: Clinical forms by S. mansoni
items.contentHtml:
Clinical forms by S. mansoni

  • S. mansoniis located in inferior mesenteric veins; eggs embolize to the liver via portal vein.
  • Intestinal form: abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, eosinophilia.
  • Chronic hepatosplenic form: Symmers' fibrosis (periportal pipe stem fibrosis) → presinusoidal portal hypertension.
  • Splenomegaly, esophageal varices, ascites; Hepatocellular function preserved until late stages.
  • DDx with alcoholic cirrhosis: in schistosomiasis the biopsy shows granulomas around eggs.

items.foco: Schistosoma haematobium causes urogenital form and hematuria
items.title: Urogenital form due to S. haematobium
items.contentHtml:
Urogenital form due to S. haematobium

  • S. haematobiumresides in the bladder and ureteral venous plexus; eggs with terminal spur.
  • Terminal hematuria (at the end of urination) is the classic symptom; It can be microscopic or macroscopic.
  • Granulomatous lesions in the bladder, ureters and genitals → fibrosis, ureteral obstruction, hydronephrosis.
  • Confirmed association with bladder squamous cell carcinoma (group 1 carcinogen).
  • In children it can cause hematuria endemic in sub-Saharan Africa.

items.foco: Swimmer's dermatitis due to cercariae penetration
items.title: swimmer's dermatitis
items.contentHtml:
Swimmer's dermatitis

  • Immediate reaction to the penetration of cercariae (from avian or human schistosomes).
  • Intense itching, erythematous macules and papules in areas exposed to fresh water, minutes to hours later.
  • In human schistosomiasis it is usually mild; in bird cercariae (non-adapted) the inflammatory reaction is more intense (self-limited).
  • It does not require antiparasitic treatment; antihistamines and topical corticosteroids relieve symptoms.
  • DDx with insect bites, hives or contact dermatitis.

items.foco: Katayama fever as an acute systemic reaction
items.title: Katayama fever
items.contentHtml:
Katayama fever

  • Acute syndrome 2-6 weeks after massive exposure to cercariae, typical in travelers (primoinfection).
  • High fever, headache, myalgia, dry cough, marked eosinophilia (>50%), lymphadenopathy.
  • Pathophysiology: type III hypersensitivity reaction (immune complexes) against migrating schistosomuli.
  • Can simulate mononucleosis, typhoid fever or sepsis; key: eosinophilia + history of bathing in fresh water in an endemic area.
  • Treatment: praziquantel 40 mg/kg/day in 2 doses; associate corticosteroids if there are severe symptoms to avoid Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction.

items.foco: Diagnosis by eggs in feces or urine depending on species
items.title: Diagnosis by eggs
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis by eggs

  • Gold standard: visualization of eggs in biological sample.
  • S. mansoni: eggs with lateral spur; They are searched for in feces by concentration methods (Kato-Katz, Ritchie).
  • S. haematobium: eggs with terminal spur; they are looked for in urine (best sample: mid-day urine or filtration).
  • Kato-Katz quantifies parasite load (eggs/gram of feces) to evaluate intensity and response to treatment.
  • Trap: eggs may not be detected in acute phases (Katayama fever); use serology (ELISA) in travelers.

items.foco: Urine filtration test for S. haematobium
items.title: Urine filtration
items.contentHtml:
Urine filtration

  • Diagnostic technique of choice for S. haematobium: filter 10 mL of urine (preferably between 10:00 and 14:00 h) with a 12-20 µm polycarbonate membrane.
  • The eggs are retained in the filter and stained with methylene blue or ninhydrin for identification.
  • Sensitivity >90% in active infections; allows quantification of load (eggs/10 mL urine).
  • Alternative: centrifuged urinary sediment, but filtration is more sensitive.
  • Useful for monitoring praziquantel effectiveness (reduction in egg excretion at 4-6 weeks post-treatment).

items.foco: Symmers fibrosis in the liver due to chronic form
items.title: Symmers fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Symmers' fibrosis

  • Also called periportal fibrosis in pipe stem (Clay pipe-stem fibrosis).
  • Secondary to egg deposition of S. mansoni in portal venules → granulomatous reaction and collagen fibrosis.
  • It affects intrahepatic portal branches, sparing hepatic parenchyma; Hepatocellular function preserved until advanced stages.
  • Image: Doppler ultrasound shows periportal echogenic thickening; Periportal hypodense bands are seen on CT or MRI.
  • Consequence: pre-sinusoidal portal hypertension with splenomegaly, esophageal varices, but without early ascites or encephalopathy.

items.foco: Portal hypertension and advanced esophageal varices
items.title: Portal hypertension and varicose veins
items.contentHtml:
Portal hypertension and esophageal varices

  • In chronic hepatosplenic schistosomiasis, portal hypertension is presinusoidal due to Symmers fibrosis.
  • Esophageal and gastric varices are the main cause of morbidity and mortality: risk of upper gastrointestinal bleeding.
  • Congestive splenomegaly, hypersplenism (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia).
  • DDx with portal hypertension due to cirrhosis: in schistosomiasis the wedged hepatic venous pressure is usually low.
  • Endoscopic management of varicose veins (band ligation) and portosystemic shunt (TIPS or surgical) in selected cases.

items.title: Praziquantel
items.contentHtml:
Praziquantel

  • Drug of choice for all Schistosoma species.
  • Dose: 40 mg/kg/day in 1-2 oral doses for 1 day (can be repeated after 2-4 weeks if high load).
  • For S. haematobium and S. mansoni: 40 mg/kg single dose; inS. japonicum or S. mekongi: 60 mg/kg in divided doses.
  • Mechanism: increases calcium permeability in the parasite membrane → paralysis and death.
  • Contraindications: pregnancy (first trimester), lactation (with caution). Adverse effects: headache, nausea, abdominal pain (mild).

items.foco: Bladder squamous cell carcinoma associated with S. haematobium
items.title: Bladder carcinoma and S. haematobium
items.contentHtml:
Bladder carcinoma and S. haematobium

  • Chronic infection by S. haematobium is an established risk factor for bladder squamous cell carcinoma (non-urothelial).
  • Pathophysiology: chronic inflammation, egg irritation, nitrosamine production and oxidative stress.
  • Clinical presentation: hematuria, dysuria, bladder mass; average age 40-50 years, earlier than urothelial carcinoma.
  • Diagnosis: cystoscopy with biopsy; histology shows keratinization and horny pearls.
  • Prevention: early antiparasitic treatment with praziquantel reduces the risk of malignant transformation.

items.foco: Prevention with snail control and avoiding contaminated fresh water
items.title: Prevention
items.contentHtml:
Prevention

  • Avoid contact with contaminated fresh water in endemic areas (bathing, washing, fishing).
  • Control of host snails: molluscicides (niclosamide), habitat modification (drainage, cementing of canals).
  • Improvement of basic sanitation: latrines, wastewater treatment to interrupt the cycle.
  • Health education: no defecate/urinate in bodies of water.
  • Chemoprophylaxis does not exist; Mass treatment with praziquantel in endemic communities reduces burden and morbidity and mortality.
